name: paracetamol
features: Fast onset of action, OTC medicine, Safe in recommended doses, No gastric irritation
uses: Mild to moderate pain; Fever reduction; Headache; Toothache; Myalgia; Post-vaccination fever
mechanism: Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis in the CNS; raises pain threshold; acts on hypothalamic heat-regulating centre to reduce fever
indications: Fever, Headache, Toothache, Muscle pain, Post-vaccination fever
dosage: Adults: 500–1000mg every 4–6h; max 4g/day. Children: 10–15mg/kg every 4–6h
timing: With or without food. Avoid alcohol.
admin_tips: Swallow tablet whole with water. Measure syrup carefully with provided syringe.
spacing: Minimum 4 hours between doses
warning_pregnancy: Considered safe in all trimesters at recommended doses. Avoid excess.
warning_pediatric: Safe from birth. Weight-based dosing: 10–15mg/kg/dose every 4–6h.
warning_driving: Does not impair driving or alertness.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C in a dry place away from light. Keep out of reach of children.
contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment, Hypersensitivity to paracetamol, Severe renal failure
interactions: Warfarin (enhanced anticoagulant effect with prolonged use), Alcohol (hepatotoxicity risk), Rifampicin (reduces paracetamol levels)
side_effects_common: Nausea (rare), Skin rash (rare), Stomach discomfort
side_effects_serious: Hepatotoxicity (overdose), Acute liver failure (overdose), Stevens-Johnson syndrome (rare)
substitutes: Ibuprofen (for inflammatory pain), Aspirin (adults for mild pain), Diclofenac (musculoskeletal pain)
pack_sizes: Strip of 15 tablets, Strip of 10 tablets, 60ml syrup, 100ml syrup
sources: WHO Essential Medicines List, Drugs.com, BNF (British National Formulary)

name: ibuprofen
features: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic, Widely available OTC, Fast pain relief, Reduces fever
uses: Pain relief; inflammation; fever; arthritis; dysmenorrhoea; dental pain; back pain
mechanism: Inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes; reduces prostaglandin synthesis; anti-inflammatory antipyretic and analgesic
indications: Mild-to-moderate pain, Fever, Osteoarthritis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Dysmenorrhoea
dosage: Adults: 200–400mg every 4–6h; max 1200mg/day OTC (2400mg with doctor supervision). Children: 5–10mg/kg every 6–8h
timing: Take with food or milk to reduce gastric irritation.
admin_tips: Take with food. Swallow tablet whole. Do not crush. Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration.
spacing: Every 6–8 hours with food
warning_pregnancy: Avoid in 3rd trimester (risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure). Use with caution in 1st-2nd trimesters.
warning_pediatric: Use cautiously in children > 6 months. Dose: 5–10mg/kg every 6–8h. Not recommended in neonates.
warning_driving: May cause drowsiness in high doses. Generally safe for driving at normal doses.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C away from direct sunlight. Keep dry.
contraindications: Active peptic ulcer, History of GI bleeding on NSAIDs, Severe renal or hepatic impairment, 3rd trimester pregnancy, Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease
interactions: Warfarin (bleeding risk), ACE inhibitors (reduced antihypertensive effect), Lithium (increased toxicity), Aspirin (GI risk), Methotrexate
side_effects_common: Dyspepsia, Nausea, Vomiting, Diarrhoea, Headache, Dizziness
side_effects_serious: GI bleeding, Peptic ulcer, Acute renal failure, Hepatotoxicity (rare), Cardiovascular events (prolonged use)
substitutes: Paracetamol, Diclofenac, Naproxen, Celecoxib (COX-2 selective), Aspirin
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, 60ml syrup, 100g gel
sources: WHO Essential Medicines List, Drugs.com, eMC (Electronic Medicines Compendium)

name: amoxicillin
features: Broad-spectrum antibiotic, Oral bioavailability good, Well tolerated, Bactericidal
uses: Bacterial infections of respiratory tract; ear; throat; urinary tract; skin; dental infections; H. pylori (triple therapy)
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs); bactericidal; active against gram-positive and some gram-negative bacteria
indications: Community-acquired pneumonia, Acute otitis media, Strep throat, Sinusitis, UTI, H. pylori eradication
dosage: Adults: 250–500mg every 8h or 500–875mg every 12h. Severe infections: up to 1g every 8h. Children: 25–50mg/kg/day divided doses
timing: Can be taken with or without food. Take at evenly spaced intervals.
admin_tips: Complete the full course even if symptoms improve. Do not miss doses.
spacing: Every 8 hours or 12 hours depending on formulation
warning_pregnancy: Generally considered safe in all trimesters. Commonly used for UTIs in pregnancy.
warning_pediatric: Safe for children. Doses based on body weight (25–50mg/kg/day). Available as flavoured syrup.
warning_driving: Does not impair driving or alertness.
warning_storage: Store capsules at room temperature below 25°C. Reconstituted syrup: refrigerate and use within 7–14 days.
contraindications: Penicillin or beta-lactam hypersensitivity, Infectious mononucleosis (risk of rash)
interactions: Warfarin (may enhance anticoagulation), Methotrexate (reduced clearance), Oral contraceptives (theoretical interaction), Probenecid (increases amoxicillin levels)
side_effects_common: Diarrhoea, Nausea, Vomiting, Skin rash, Headache
side_effects_serious: Severe allergic reaction / anaphylaxis, Clostridioides difficile colitis, Severe skin reactions (SJS/TEN)
substitutes: Ampicillin, Co-amoxiclav (Augmentin), Azithromycin, Doxycycline, Cefuroxime
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 capsules, 60ml syrup, IV vials
sources: WHO Essential Medicines List, BNF, Drugs.com

name: metformin
features: First-line for type 2 diabetes, Weight-neutral or promotes mild weight loss, No hypoglycaemia risk alone, Cardioprotective
uses: Type 2 diabetes mellitus; polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS); prediabetes; insulin resistance
mechanism: Reduces hepatic glucose production (inhibits gluconeogenesis via AMPK pathway); improves insulin sensitivity in muscles; reduces intestinal glucose absorption
indications: Type 2 diabetes (first-line), PCOS, Prediabetes, Insulin resistance
dosage: Adults: Start 500mg twice daily with meals; increase gradually to 1500–2000mg/day in divided doses. Max 2550mg/day.
timing: Take with meals to minimise GI side effects. Do not crush extended-release tablets.
admin_tips: Swallow whole. Start low and titrate slowly. Monitor renal function regularly.
spacing: Twice or three times daily with main meals
warning_pregnancy: Generally avoided in first trimester; insulin preferred for gestational diabetes. May be used in some cases under specialist guidance.
warning_pediatric: Not approved for children under 10 years. Used in some adolescents with type 2 diabetes: 500–2000mg/day.
warning_driving: Does not impair alertness. May require monitoring of blood glucose before driving.
warning_storage: Store at room temperature below 30°C in a dry place. Keep tightly closed.
contraindications: eGFR < 30 mL/min (contraindicated), Lactic acidosis risk conditions, Hepatic impairment, Radiological contrast: hold 48h, Alcoholism, Heart failure requiring pharmacotherapy
interactions: Alcohol (lactic acidosis risk), Iodinated contrast agents (hold metformin), Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (lactic acidosis), Topiramate
side_effects_common: GI upset, Nausea, Diarrhoea, Metallic taste, Abdominal discomfort (especially when starting)
side_effects_serious: Lactic acidosis (rare but serious, especially with renal impairment), Vitamin B12 deficiency (long-term use)
substitutes: Empagliflozin, Sitagliptin, Glipizide, Pioglitazone, Insulin
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, Strip of 30 tablets
sources: ADA Diabetes Standards of Care 2023, BNF, Drugs.com

name: hypromellose
brand_names: Eyemist, Tears Naturale, Lacri-Lube, Refresh, Visine Tears
features: Lubricates dry eyes, No preservatives in single-dose units, Viscosity-forming agent, OTC/self-care
uses: Dry eye syndrome; temporary relief of eye irritation; ocular lubricant post-surgery or lens wear
mechanism: Forms a thin viscous layer over the ocular surface; replaces and supplements deficient tear film; reduces evaporation and friction; soothes irritated corneal surface
indications: Dry eye syndrome, Keratoconjunctivitis sicca, Post-surgical ocular lubrication, Computer vision syndrome
dosage: 1–2 drops into each affected eye 3–4 times daily or more as needed. Adjust frequency to symptom severity.
timing: Can be used at any time. Remove contact lenses before instilling; wait 15 minutes before reinserting.
admin_tips: Tilt head back; pull lower eyelid down; instil drops into conjunctival sac. Blink gently. Do not touch dropper tip to eye.
spacing: 3–4 times daily or as needed for symptomatic relief
warning_pregnancy: Safe for use during pregnancy. No systemic absorption of clinical significance.
warning_pediatric: Safe for children from birth. Used in neonatal intensive care as ocular lubricant.
warning_driving: Does not affect vision significantly. Transient blurring possible immediately after use.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C protected from light. Discard single-dose vials immediately after use. Multi-dose: use within 30 days of opening.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to hypromellose or any component of the formulation
interactions: No significant drug interactions. Compatibility with other eye drops: separate instillation by 5 minutes.
side_effects_common: Transient blurring immediately after instillation, Mild stinging or burning (rare)
side_effects_serious: Severe persistent eye pain or redness (seek medical attention), Allergic conjunctivitis (discontinue)
substitutes: Carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) drops, Polyvinyl alcohol drops, Sodium hyaluronate gel, Dextran eye drops
pack_sizes: Single-dose vials (box of 30), Multi-dose bottle 10ml, Multi-dose bottle 15ml
sources: FDA Drug Label, Drugs.com, British National Formulary

name: cetirizine
features: Non-sedating antihistamine, Once daily dosing, Rapid onset within 1 hour, Minimal anticholinergic effects
uses: Allergic rhinitis (seasonal and perennial); urticaria (hives); allergic conjunctivitis; angioedema
mechanism: Selective H1 receptor antagonist; blocks histamine action on effector cells; reduces symptoms of allergic response including itching sneezing and rash; low CNS penetration = less sedation
indications: Seasonal allergic rhinitis, Perennial allergic rhinitis, Urticaria, Allergic conjunctivitis, Atopic dermatitis
dosage: Adults and children >6 years: 10mg once daily. Children 2–6 years: 5mg once daily or 2.5mg twice daily. Renal impairment: reduce dose.
timing: Can be taken with or without food. Take at the same time each day.
admin_tips: Swallow tablet whole with water. Syrup: measure accurately with provided dosing cup.
spacing: Once daily (preferably in the evening to avoid any residual drowsiness)
warning_pregnancy: Limited data; generally avoided in 1st trimester; use only if clearly needed with physician guidance.
warning_pediatric: Safe from 2 years of age. Children 2–5: 2.5mg twice daily or 5mg once daily. Children > 6: 10mg once daily.
warning_driving: May cause mild drowsiness in some individuals. Avoid driving if affected.
warning_storage: Store at room temperature below 30°C. Keep away from moisture and heat.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to cetirizine or hydroxyzine, Severe renal failure (eGFR < 10)
interactions: CNS depressants (additive sedation), Alcohol (avoid), Anticholinergics (additive effects), Ritonavir (increased cetirizine levels)
side_effects_common: Drowsiness (less than first-gen antihistamines), Dry mouth, Headache, Fatigue
side_effects_serious: Severe hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis (rare), QT prolongation (very rare at high doses)
substitutes: Loratadine, Fexofenadine, Levocetirizine, Desloratadine, Bilastine
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, 30ml syrup, 60ml syrup
sources: Drugs.com, BNF, WHO Model Formulary

name: omeprazole
features: Once daily dosing, Effective acid suppression, Available OTC and Rx, Reduces risk of GI bleeding
uses: Gastric ulcer; duodenal ulcer; GERD; H. pylori eradication (triple therapy); Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
mechanism: Irreversibly binds and inhibits H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) in gastric parietal cells; reduces gastric acid secretion by up to 95%
indications: GERD, Peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori (triple therapy), Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
dosage: Adults: 20–40mg once daily before meal. H. pylori eradication: 20mg twice daily for 7–14 days (triple therapy). Max 40mg/day for GERD.
timing: Take 30–60 minutes before the first meal of the day for maximum efficacy.
admin_tips: Swallow capsule whole. Do not crush. If difficulty swallowing open capsule and mix pellets with acidic food (applesauce).
spacing: Once daily before breakfast (standard) or twice daily (H. pylori triple therapy)
warning_pregnancy: Use with caution — generally considered low risk (Category C); benefits may outweigh risks under physician supervision.
warning_pediatric: Safe from age 1 year for GERD. Paediatric dose: 0.5–1mg/kg/day once daily; max 20mg/day.
warning_driving: Does not impair driving ability.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C in original packaging. Protect from moisture and light.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to omeprazole or other PPIs, Concurrent rilpivirine use
interactions: Clopidogrel (reduced antiplatelet effect; use pantoprazole instead), Methotrexate (increased toxicity), Digoxin (increased levels), Ketoconazole
side_effects_common: Headache, Nausea, Diarrhoea, Abdominal pain, Flatulence
side_effects_serious: C. difficile infection (long-term use), Hypomagnesaemia (chronic use), Bone fractures (long-term high dose)
substitutes: Pantoprazole, Lansoprazole, Rabeprazole, Esomeprazole, Ranitidine (H2 blocker)
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 capsules, Strip of 14 capsules, Pack of 28 capsules
sources: FDA Drug Label, BNF, WHO Essential Medicines List

name: azithromycin
features: Once daily dosing, Short 3-5 day courses, Broad-spectrum, Long tissue half-life
uses: Community-acquired pneumonia; pharyngitis; urethritis; skin infections; pelvic inflammatory disease; MAC prophylaxis
mechanism: Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding to 50S ribosomal subunit; bacteriostatic; concentrated in tissues (long half-life); active against most atypical organisms
indications: Community-acquired pneumonia, Acute pharyngitis, STIs (Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea), Skin infections, Otitis media
dosage: Adults: 500mg on day 1 then 250mg once daily for 4 days (5-day course) OR 500mg once daily for 3 days. Children: 10mg/kg on day 1 then 5mg/kg for 4 days.
timing: Take with or without food. Antacids may delay absorption — separate by 2 hours.
admin_tips: Complete the full prescribed course. Report symptoms of liver problems.
spacing: Once daily for 3–5 days
warning_pregnancy: Generally avoided — limited safety data. Use only if clearly indicated under specialist advice.
warning_pediatric: Safe in children from 6 months. Weight-based dosing. Oral suspension available.
warning_driving: Avoid driving if dizziness occurs.
warning_storage: Store tablets below 30°C. Reconstituted suspension: store in refrigerator and use within 10 days.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to azithromycin or macrolides, Known QT prolongation, Severe hepatic impairment
interactions: Warfarin (increased INR), QT-prolonging drugs (additive QT prolongation), Antacids (reduced absorption), Digoxin
side_effects_common: Diarrhoea, Nausea, Abdominal pain, Vomiting (less than erythromycin)
side_effects_serious: Hepatotoxicity, QT prolongation/arrhythmia, Clostridioides difficile colitis, Severe allergic reactions
substitutes: Clarithromycin, Doxycycline, Amoxicillin-clavulanate, Levofloxacin
pack_sizes: Strip of 3 tablets, Strip of 5 tablets, 30ml syrup, 60ml syrup
sources: WHO Essential Medicines List, BNF, Drugs.com

name: amlodipine
features: Once daily dosing, Long acting, No first-dose hypotension, Well tolerated
uses: Hypertension (high blood pressure); stable and vasospastic angina; coronary artery disease
mechanism: Blocks L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle and cardiac cells; reduces peripheral vascular resistance; dilates coronary arteries; reduces myocardial oxygen demand
indications: Hypertension, Stable angina, Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, Coronary artery disease
dosage: Adults: Start 5mg once daily. May increase to 10mg once daily. Elderly or hepatic impairment: start 2.5mg.
timing: Can be taken at any time with or without food. Take at the same time each day.
admin_tips: Swallow whole. Do not stop abruptly without medical advice.
spacing: Once daily at the same time each day
warning_pregnancy: Avoid in 2nd and 3rd trimester — risk of fetal harm. ACE inhibitors or methyldopa preferred for hypertension in pregnancy.
warning_pediatric: Not generally recommended under 6 years. Adolescents 6–17: 2.5–5mg once daily.
warning_driving: May cause dizziness — caution particularly when starting therapy.
warning_storage: Store below 30°C protected from light and moisture.
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to amlodipine or dihydropyridines, Cardiogenic shock, Severe aortic stenosis
interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin — increase amlodipine levels), Simvastatin (amlodipine raises simvastatin levels; cap simvastatin at 20mg), Cyclosporine
side_effects_common: Peripheral oedema (ankle swelling), Flushing, Headache, Dizziness, Palpitations
side_effects_serious: Severe hypotension, Reflex tachycardia, Hepatotoxicity (rare), Gynaecomastia (rare)
substitutes: Nifedipine, Felodipine, Lercanidipine, Ramipril (ACE inhibitor), Losartan (ARB)
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, Pack of 30 tablets
sources: JNC 8 Hypertension Guidelines, BNF, Drugs.com

name: atorvastatin
features: Once daily dosing, High-intensity statin, Reduces cardiovascular risk, Pleiotropic effects
uses: High cholesterol (hypercholesterolaemia); mixed dyslipidaemia; prevention of cardiovascular events (MI, stroke) in high-risk patients
mechanism: Competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis); reduces LDL-C by 25–55%; increases HDL-C; pleiotropic anti-inflammatory effects
indications: Primary hypercholesterolaemia, Mixed dyslipidaemia, Cardiovascular risk reduction, Post-MI secondary prevention
dosage: Adults: 10–80mg once daily. Starting dose usually 10–20mg. High-risk patients: 40–80mg once daily.
timing: Can be taken at any time of day with or without food. Consistent daily timing preferred.
admin_tips: Do not take with grapefruit juice. Report any muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness to your doctor.
spacing: Once daily at any time
warning_pregnancy: Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy — risk of fetal harm (cholesterol essential for fetal development). Use effective contraception.
warning_pediatric: Not recommended for children under 10 years. Children 10–17: 10–20mg/day (specialist only).
warning_driving: Does not impair driving. Report any muscle weakness or pain.
warning_storage: Store below 30°C in a dry place away from direct sunlight.
contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (absolute contraindication), Active liver disease, Unexplained persistent elevated serum transaminases
interactions: CYP3A4 inhibitors (clarithromycin, itraconazole — increase statin levels; increased myopathy risk), Gemfibrozil (avoid combination), Cyclosporine, Niacin (myopathy risk), Colchicine
side_effects_common: Myalgia (muscle pain — most common), Gastrointestinal upset, Headache, Elevated transaminases (mild)
side_effects_serious: Rhabdomyolysis (rare but serious — report severe muscle pain), Severe hepatotoxicity, Immune-mediated necrotising myopathy
substitutes: Rosuvastatin, Simvastatin, Pravastatin, Ezetimibe (different mechanism), PCSK9 inhibitors
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, Pack of 30 tablets
sources: ACC/AHA Cholesterol Guidelines, BNF, Drugs.com

name: pantoprazole
brand_names: Pantop, Protonix, Pan D, Pantocid, Pansa
features: Fewer drug interactions than omeprazole, Enteric-coated tablet, Safe in patients on clopidogrel, Once daily dosing
uses: GERD; peptic ulcer disease; H. pylori eradication; hypersecretory conditions; Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+ ATPase proton pump in gastric parietal cells; superior drug interaction profile compared to omeprazole due to less CYP2C19 dependence
indications: GERD, Peptic ulcer disease, Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, H. pylori (triple therapy), Long-term acid-related conditions
dosage: Adults: 20–40mg once daily before breakfast. H. pylori: 40mg twice daily x 7–14 days. ZES: higher doses may be needed.
timing: Take 30–60 minutes before a meal. Swallow enteric-coated tablet whole.
admin_tips: Do not crush or chew enteric-coated tablet. Swallow whole with water.
spacing: Once daily before breakfast
warning_pregnancy: Use with caution; Category B — generally considered safer for GI conditions in pregnancy than omeprazole under physician guidance.
warning_pediatric: Safety in children under 5 not well established. Children 5–16: 20–40mg daily for GERD under specialist care.
warning_driving: Does not impair driving.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C protected from moisture and light.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to pantoprazole or any PPI, Concurrent HIV protease inhibitor use (atazanavir/nelfinavir)
interactions: Atazanavir (avoid — PPI reduces absorption), Warfarin (monitor INR), Methotrexate (increased concentration), Ketoconazole (reduced absorption)
side_effects_common: Headache, Diarrhoea, Nausea, Abdominal pain, Flatulence
side_effects_serious: Clostridioides difficile infection, Hypomagnesaemia, Vitamin B12 deficiency (long-term use), Bone fractures (long-term)
substitutes: Omeprazole, Lansoprazole, Rabeprazole, Esomeprazole, Ranitidine (H2 blocker)
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, IV vials
sources: FDA Drug Label, BNF, Drugs.com

name: dextromethorphan
brand_names: Robitussin DX, Alex, Benadryl DX, Honitus, Drosyn
features: Non-narcotic, No opioid dependence risk, Effective for dry irritating cough, Available OTC
uses: Temporary relief of dry non-productive cough; cough associated with upper respiratory tract infections; common cold
mechanism: NMDA receptor antagonist and sigma-1 receptor agonist; acts centrally on the cough centre in the medulla oblongata; suppresses cough reflex without producing analgesia
indications: Non-productive cough due to URTIs, Common cold, Flu, Post-infectious cough
dosage: Adults: 10–20mg every 4h or 30mg every 6–8h; max 120mg/day. Children 6–12: 5–10mg every 4h; max 60mg/day.
timing: Take with water. Can be taken with or without food. Nighttime dosing helps cough during sleep.
admin_tips: Do not use longer than 7 days without medical advice. Not for productive (chesty) cough.
spacing: Every 4–6 hours as needed
warning_pregnancy: Avoid in 1st trimester — limited data. Consult physician. Safer alternatives preferred in pregnancy.
warning_pediatric: Not for children under 6 years without medical supervision. Children under 4: avoid.
warning_driving: Can cause drowsiness — avoid driving or operating machinery. High doses may cause dissociation.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C away from heat and sunlight. Keep out of reach of children.
contraindications: MAO inhibitor use within 14 days (serotonin syndrome risk), Hypersensitivity to dextromethorphan
interactions: MAOIs (serotonin syndrome — contraindicated), SSRIs (serotonin syndrome risk — caution), CNS depressants (additive sedation), Quinidine (increases DXM levels)
side_effects_common: Drowsiness, Dizziness, Nausea, Constipation, Stomach upset
side_effects_serious: Serotonin syndrome (with MAOIs or SSRIs), Respiratory depression (overdose), Dysphoria and dissociation (abuse doses)
substitutes: Codeine syrup (Rx only), Guaifenesin, Honey (mild cough), Benzonatate, Levocloperastine
pack_sizes: 60ml syrup, 100ml syrup, Strip of 10 tablets, Pack of 10 lozenges
sources: FDA Drug Label, Drugs.com, BNF

name: losartan
features: Well tolerated, No dry cough (unlike ACE inhibitors), Uricosuric (lowers uric acid), Once daily, Renal protective in diabetes
uses: Hypertension; diabetic nephropathy; heart failure; stroke prevention in hypertensive patients with LVH
mechanism: Selectively blocks AT1 subtype of angiotensin II receptor; prevents vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion; reduces BP and cardiac afterload; renal protective in diabetic nephropathy
indications: Hypertension, Diabetic nephropathy, Heart failure (as add-on), Stroke prevention (LVH), Hyperuricaemia with hypertension
dosage: Adults: Start 50mg once daily. Range 25–100mg once daily. Heart failure add-on: 12.5–50mg.
timing: Can be taken with or without food. Take at the same time each day.
admin_tips: Monitor potassium and renal function regularly. Avoid potassium supplements without medical advice.
spacing: Once daily at same time each day
warning_pregnancy: Absolutely contraindicated in 2nd and 3rd trimester — risk of fetal renal damage and death. ARBs are teratogenic.
warning_pediatric: Safety in children under 6 not established. Children 6–17 with hypertension: 0.7mg/kg/day (max 50mg).
warning_driving: May cause dizziness especially initially. Caution on driving until effect known.
warning_storage: Store below 30°C away from moisture and heat.
contraindications: Bilateral renal artery stenosis, Pregnancy (2nd/3rd trimester — absolute), Hypersensitivity to losartan, Concurrent aliskiren use with diabetes or renal impairment
interactions: ACE inhibitors (additive hypotension and hyperkalaemia), Potassium-sparing diuretics (hyperkalaemia), NSAIDs (reduced antihypertensive effect and renal risk), Lithium
side_effects_common: Dizziness, Hypotension (especially first dose), Hyperkalaemia, Fatigue, Headache
side_effects_serious: Acute kidney injury, Severe hyperkalaemia, Angioedema (rare but possible), First-dose hypotension
substitutes: Valsartan, Telmisartan, Olmesartan, Ramipril (ACE inhibitor), Amlodipine
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, Pack of 30 tablets
sources: JNC 8 Hypertension Guidelines, BNF, ACC/AHA Heart Guidelines

name: aspirin
brand_names: Ecosprin, Disprin, Aspro, Loprin, Aspirin Cardio
features: Dual analgesic and antiplatelet, Low dose cardioprotective, Widely available, Irreversible COX inhibition
uses: Antiplatelet: prevention of MI and stroke; acute MI (loading dose); Analgesic: headache mild pain; Antipyretic (less used now); Anti-inflammatory (high dose)
mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 via acetylation; at low doses inhibits thromboxane A2 in platelets (antiplatelet); at high doses inhibits prostaglandins (anti-inflammatory analgesic)
indications: Acute MI (STEMI), Secondary prevention of MI and stroke, Stable angina, TIA prevention
dosage: Antiplatelet: 75–100mg once daily (low dose). Acute MI: 300mg loading dose. Analgesic/antipyretic: 300–600mg every 4–6h.
timing: Antiplatelet dose: with or after food. Analgesic dose: with milk or food to reduce gastric irritation.
admin_tips: Dispersible tablets: dissolve in water. Enteric-coated: swallow whole. Do not give to children under 16 (Reye syndrome risk).
spacing: Once daily for antiplatelet; every 4–6h for pain
warning_pregnancy: Avoid in 3rd trimester — risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure. Low-dose may be used in selected high-risk patients under specialist supervision.
warning_pediatric: Do NOT give to children under 16 years (risk of Reye syndrome — rare but fatal liver-brain disease).
warning_driving: May cause dizziness or GI discomfort. Generally safe for driving.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C in a dry place away from moisture. Do not use if tablets smell strongly of vinegar (acetic acid).
contraindications: Active peptic ulceration, Bleeding disorders (haemophilia), Hypersensitivity to aspirin, Children under 16 (Reye syndrome), 3rd trimester pregnancy, Severe hepatic impairment
interactions: Warfarin and anticoagulants (major bleeding risk), NSAIDs (increased GI bleeding), ACE inhibitors (reduced effect), Methotrexate (increased toxicity), Valproate
side_effects_common: GI irritation, Nausea, Dyspepsia, Tinnitus (high doses)
side_effects_serious: GI bleeding, Peptic ulcer, Reye syndrome (children under 16), Haemorrhagic stroke (at anticoagulant doses), Bronchospasm (aspirin-sensitive asthma)
substitutes: Clopidogrel, Ticagrelor, Prasugrel (antiplatelet alternatives), Ibuprofen (analgesic), Paracetamol
pack_sizes: Strip of 14 tablets, Strip of 30 tablets, Dispersible strip of 28
sources: ACC/AHA Antiplatelet Guidelines, BNF, WHO Essential Medicines List

name: montelukast
features: Once daily (evening) dosing, No inhaler technique required, Oral formulation, Anti-leukotriene and anti-allergic
uses: Asthma prophylaxis (not for acute attacks); seasonal allergic rhinitis; perennial allergic rhinitis; exercise-induced bronchoconstriction
mechanism: Selectively blocks cysteinyl leukotriene CysLT1 receptor; prevents leukotriene-mediated bronchoconstriction and mucus secretion; reduces airway inflammation; inhibits allergic rhinitis symptoms
indications: Asthma (add-on to ICS), Allergic rhinitis, Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction
dosage: Adults and adolescents > 15 years: 10mg once daily in the evening. Children 6–14: 5mg chewable once daily. Children 2–5: 4mg chewable/granules once daily.
timing: Take in the evening (particularly for asthma). Can be taken with or without food.
admin_tips: Swallow tablet whole. Chewable tablets: chew thoroughly before swallowing. Granules: mix with soft food.
spacing: Once daily in the evening
warning_pregnancy: Limited data — generally avoid in 1st trimester. Inhaled corticosteroids preferred for asthma in pregnancy.
warning_pediatric: Safe from 6 months (granules). Age-appropriate formulations available (granules for infants, chewable for children 2–14).
warning_driving: Does not impair driving. However patient should be aware of neuropsychiatric events (rare).
warning_storage: Store below 30°C protected from moisture and light. Keep granules sachets sealed until use.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to montelukast. Not for acute asthma attacks or bronchospasm episodes.
interactions: Phenobarbital (may reduce montelukast levels by enzyme induction), Rifampicin, Gemfibrozil (increases montelukast exposure)
side_effects_common: Headache, Abdominal pain, Thirst, Diarrhoea, Elevated liver enzymes (mild)
side_effects_serious: Neuropsychiatric events (agitation, depression, suicidal ideation — FDA black box warning; rare), Severe hepatic reactions
substitutes: Beclometasone inhaler (ICS only), Salbutamol (reliever), Formoterol (LABA), Cetirizine (for rhinitis component), Zafirlukast
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 14 tablets, Box of 30 sachets (granules)
sources: FDA Drug Label, BNF, GINA Asthma Guidelines

name: salbutamol
features: Rapid onset within 5 minutes (inhaler), Short-acting rescue medication, Well established safety profile, Available in multiple formulations
uses: Acute relief of bronchospasm in asthma and COPD; exercise-induced bronchoconstriction; acute severe asthma (nebulisation); premature labour (off-label)
mechanism: Selective beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist; relaxes bronchial smooth muscle; produces bronchodilation; increases mucociliary clearance; rapid onset 5 min peak 15–30 min duration 4–6h
indications: Acute asthma attack, COPD exacerbation, Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction, Acute bronchospasm
dosage: Inhaler: 1–2 puffs (100–200mcg) every 4–6h as needed. Acute attack: 4–8 puffs via spacer. Nebuliser: 2.5mg every 20 min (acute). Oral: 2–4mg 3–4 times daily.
timing: Inhaler use as needed for symptoms. Tablet: with food if GI upset.
admin_tips: Shake inhaler before use. Use spacer device for better lung deposition. Rinse mouth after if using ICS together.
spacing: Every 4–6 hours as needed (or as directed); more frequent use indicates poor asthma control
warning_pregnancy: Safe for use in all trimesters. Essential rescue medication for acute asthma — do not withhold.
warning_pediatric: Safe for children from 4 years via inhaler with spacer. Dose: 1–2 puffs as needed.
warning_driving: May cause tremor or palpitations. Rarely impairs driving.
warning_storage: Store inhaler below 30°C. Do not puncture or incinerate canister. Protect from frost.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to salbutamol, Tachyarrhythmia
interactions: Non-selective beta blockers (antagonise effect — avoid), MAOIs and tricyclic antidepressants (cardiovascular effects), Digoxin (hypokalaemia risk with high-dose salbutamol), Theophylline
side_effects_common: Tremor, Palpitations, Headache, Tachycardia, Hypokalaemia (high doses)
side_effects_serious: Severe hypokalaemia (high dose nebulisation), Paradoxical bronchospasm (rare), Cardiac arrhythmia (overdose)
substitutes: Terbutaline, Formoterol (LABA), Ipratropium (for COPD), Levalbuterol (R-enantiomer of salbutamol)
pack_sizes: Inhaler (200 doses), Spacer device kit, Nebuliser solution 2.5ml vials (pack of 20), Tablets strip of 10
sources: GINA Asthma Guidelines, BNF, WHO Essential Medicines List

name: diclofenac
features: Anti-inflammatory and analgesic, Topical gel available for local application, Injectable form for severe pain, Selective COX-2 preference (more GI-friendly than ibuprofen)
uses: Mild to moderate pain; osteoarthritis; rheumatoid arthritis; ankylosing spondylitis; musculoskeletal pain; post-operative pain; dysmenorrhoea
mechanism: Inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes with slight COX-2 selectivity; reduces prostaglandin synthesis; anti-inflammatory analgesic and antipyretic
indications: Osteoarthritis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Acute musculoskeletal pain, Post-operative pain, Dysmenorrhoea
dosage: Adults: 75–150mg/day in 2–3 divided doses. SR tablets: 75–100mg once or twice daily. Topical gel: apply to affected area 3–4 times daily.
timing: Take with food or milk to reduce gastric side effects. SR tablets can be taken once or twice daily.
admin_tips: Apply gel with gentle massage. Wash hands after application. Do not apply to broken skin or wounds.
spacing: Two to three times daily (standard); once or twice daily (slow-release)
warning_pregnancy: Avoid in 3rd trimester (premature closure of ductus arteriosus). 1st and 2nd trimester: only if essential.
warning_pediatric: Avoid in children under 1 year for systemic use. Paediatric suppositories used for fever in some countries.
warning_driving: May cause dizziness or visual disturbances. Caution on driving.
warning_storage: Store tablets below 25°C. Gel: store below 30°C. Keep tightly closed.
contraindications: Active GI ulcer or bleeding, Severe hepatic impairment, Severe renal impairment, 3rd trimester pregnancy, Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease, Post-CABG pain
interactions: Warfarin (bleeding risk), Lithium (increased toxicity), Methotrexate (increased toxicity), ACE inhibitors (reduced effect and renal risk), Cyclosporine (nephrotoxicity)
side_effects_common: GI irritation, Nausea, Dyspepsia, Headache, Dizziness (topical: local skin irritation)
side_effects_serious: GI bleeding, Peptic ulcer, Hepatotoxicity, Cardiovascular events (long-term use), Acute renal failure
substitutes: Ibuprofen, Naproxen, Celecoxib, Etoricoxib, Aceclofenac
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 10 SR tablets, 50g gel tube, Ampoules 75mg/3ml
sources: Drugs.com, BNF, eMC

name: clonazepam
features: Long-acting benzodiazepine, Effective for absence and myoclonic seizures, Also used for panic disorder and anxiety, Reduces seizure duration and frequency
uses: Epilepsy (absence seizures, myoclonic seizures, Lennox-Gastaut syndrome); panic disorder; agoraphobia; status epilepticus (IV); restless legs syndrome
mechanism: Potentiates GABA-A receptor activity by binding to benzodiazepine site; increases chloride ion influx; hyperpolarises neurons; reduces neuronal excitability and seizure threshold
indications: Absence seizures, Myoclonic epilepsy, Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, Panic disorder, Restless legs
dosage: Epilepsy — Adults: 0.5mg three times daily increasing gradually to 8–10mg/day divided doses. Children: 0.01–0.05mg/kg/day divided three times daily. Panic disorder: 0.25–1mg twice daily.
timing: Take at the same times each day. Can be taken with or without food. Do not stop abruptly.
admin_tips: Do not stop abruptly — taper gradually to avoid withdrawal seizures. Avoid alcohol. Report any mood changes.
spacing: Two to three times daily (epilepsy); twice daily (panic disorder)
warning_pregnancy: Avoid — risk of neonatal withdrawal syndrome, neonatal hypotonia, and respiratory depression.
warning_pediatric: Used in epilepsy from birth under neurologist guidance. Dose strictly by weight.
warning_driving: Significantly impairs driving and operating machinery. Do not drive while on this medication.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C protected from light and moisture. Schedule H / Controlled substance — keep secure.
contraindications: Severe respiratory insufficiency, Sleep apnoea syndrome, Myasthenia gravis, Acute narrow-angle glaucoma, Alcohol dependence
interactions: CNS depressants and alcohol (additive sedation — serious), Opioids (respiratory depression — avoid or use with extreme caution), Carbamazepine (reduces clonazepam levels), Valproate
side_effects_common: Drowsiness, Sedation, Ataxia, Dizziness, Memory impairment, Hypersalivation
side_effects_serious: Respiratory depression (with opioids or alcohol), Severe sedation, Paradoxical effects (agitation), Physical and psychological dependence, Withdrawal seizures on abrupt cessation
substitutes: Valproate (for epilepsy), Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, Diazepam (shorter-acting BZD), Lorazepam
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, 10ml oral drops
sources: NICE Epilepsy Guidelines, BNF, Drugs.com

name: aceclofenac
features: Claimed preferential COX-2 inhibition, Lower GI side effects than diclofenac, Twice daily dosing, Comparable efficacy to diclofenac
uses: Osteoarthritis; rheumatoid arthritis; ankylosing spondylitis; acute musculoskeletal pain; dysmenorrhoea; dental pain
mechanism: Partially converted to diclofenac in the body; inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 but with some COX-2 preference; inhibits prostaglandin E2 and interleukin-1 beta; significant anti-inflammatory and analgesic activity
indications: Osteoarthritis, Rheumatoid arthritis, Ankylosing spondylitis, Musculoskeletal pain, Dental pain
dosage: Adults: 100mg twice daily with food. Continue only as long as needed; use lowest effective dose.
timing: Take with food or milk to reduce GI side effects.
admin_tips: Complete the prescribed course. Do not take more than 200mg/day. Monitor for GI symptoms.
spacing: Twice daily with meals
warning_pregnancy: Avoid in 3rd trimester. Avoid in 1st and 2nd trimester unless clearly necessary.
warning_pediatric: Not recommended in children (limited paediatric data).
warning_driving: May cause mild dizziness. Generally safe for driving.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C in a dry place away from heat and light.
contraindications: Active peptic ulcer, GI bleeding, Severe renal or hepatic impairment, 3rd trimester pregnancy, Aspirin-sensitive asthma
interactions: Warfarin (bleeding risk), Lithium (increased toxicity), Digoxin (increased levels), ACE inhibitors, Methotrexate
side_effects_common: GI discomfort, Nausea, Dyspepsia, Elevated liver enzymes, Skin rash
side_effects_serious: GI bleeding, Peptic ulceration, Hepatotoxicity (rare), Acute renal failure, Serious skin reactions
substitutes: Diclofenac, Ibuprofen, Celecoxib, Etoricoxib, Paracetamol
pack_sizes: Strip of 10 tablets, Strip of 15 tablets, 50g gel tube
sources: Drugs.com, BNF, CIMS India

name: cholecalciferol
brand_names: Calcirol, D3 Shots, Uprise D3, Arachitol, Sunny D3
features: Essential vitamin with hormone-like activity, Immunomodulatory effects, Deficiency extremely common in India, Safe wide therapeutic range
uses: Vitamin D deficiency; osteoporosis prevention and treatment; rickets; hypoparathyroidism; immune support; osteomalacia
mechanism: Converted to calcidiol in liver and then to calcitriol in kidneys; calcitriol binds VDR nuclear receptors; regulates calcium and phosphate absorption; essential for bone mineralisation and immune function
indications: Vitamin D deficiency, Osteoporosis (with calcium), Rickets, Hypoparathyroidism, Immune support
dosage: Loading: 60000 IU weekly x 8 weeks (deficiency). Maintenance: 1000–2000 IU daily or 60000 IU monthly. IM injection: 600000 IU once or twice yearly.
timing: Take with a fatty meal for optimal absorption (fat-soluble vitamin). Weekly or monthly based on formulation.
admin_tips: Do not exceed recommended doses. Monitor serum 25-OH-D3 levels. Ensure adequate calcium intake alongside supplementation.
spacing: Once weekly (loading); once monthly (maintenance); or once daily (low dose maintenance)
warning_pregnancy: Safe during pregnancy — essential for fetal bone development and maternal health. Standard supplement in antenatal care.
warning_pediatric: Safe from birth. Deficiency common in exclusively breastfed infants after 2 months — supplement 400 IU/day.
warning_driving: Doses within recommended range do not impair driving.
warning_storage: Store below 25°C protected from light. Injection: refrigerate. Sachets: store away from moisture.
contraindications: Hypercalcaemia, Hypercalciuria, Vitamin D toxicity, Hypersensitivity to vitamin D preparations, Sarcoidosis
interactions: Thiazide diuretics (hypercalcaemia risk), Digoxin (hypercalcaemia potentiates toxicity), Orlistat (reduces absorption), Anticonvulsants (may reduce vitamin D levels)
side_effects_common: Nausea (high doses), Constipation, Fatigue, Hypercalciuria (mild)
side_effects_serious: Hypercalcaemia (toxicity — weakness, confusion, polyuria), Nephrocalcinosis (very high doses), Soft tissue calcification
substitutes: Ergocalciferol (Vitamin D2), Calcitriol, Alfacalcidol (activated D3), Combined calcium and D3 supplements
pack_sizes: Capsule strip of 4 (60000 IU), Sachet box of 4, 1ml injection vial
sources: ICMR Guidelines Vitamin D 2023, BNF, Drugs.com
